items.question: What is σ(0)?
items.explanation: σ(0) = 1/(1+e^0) = 1/2 = 0.5 — the neutral midpoint of the sigmoid curve.

items.question: What is the maximum value of σ′(x) = σ(x)(1−σ(x))?
items.explanation: The derivative peaks at x=0 where σ(0)=0.5, giving 0.5×0.5=0.25. This cap drives vanishing gradients in deep sigmoid stacks.

items.question: For large positive x, σ(x) and σ′(x) behave as:
items.explanation: Large x pushes output toward 1 on the flat upper tail; the slope σ′(x) approaches zero — classic saturation.

items.question: Which layer role is sigmoid still a good default for?
options: All 20 hidden layers in an MLP
items.explanation: Sigmoid maps logits to (0,1) for P(y=1|x). Deep hidden layers should use ReLU to avoid vanishing gradients.

items.question: Ten saturated sigmoid hidden layers each contribute σ′≈0.25 at most. The cumulative gradient factor is at most:
items.explanation: Backprop multiplies per-layer σ′ factors. Even at the maximum 0.25 per layer, 0.25¹⁰ ≈ 9.5×10⁻⁷ — illustrating vanishing gradients.

items.question: Sigmoid output range for finite real inputs is:
items.explanation: For any finite x, 0 < σ(x) < 1. Endpoints 0 and 1 are limits as x→±∞, not values actually attained.
